title: Improving Algorithmic Efficiency Using Cryptography
speaker: Or Zamir
abstract:
Cryptographic primitives have been used for various non-cryptographic objectives, such as eliminating or reducing randomness and interaction. We show how to use cryptography to improve the time complexity of solving computational problems. Specifically, we show that under standard cryptographic assumptions, we can design algorithms that are asymptotically faster than existing ones while maintaining correctness.
We introduce and construct "Trapdoor Matrix Distributions", using which, we present the first uniform reduction from worst-case to approximate and average-case matrix multiplication with optimal parameters (improving on HS2025, albeit under computational assumptions), the first WC to average-case reductions for matrix inversion and other linear operations, fast general-purpose dimension reductions, as well as a speedup of inference time in classification models.
Based on joint work with Vinod Vaikuntanathan.

proof_stages.label: Computational indistinguishability as the primitive
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the goal as using cryptographic indistinguishability, rather than privacy or security per se, to affect basic algorithmic running times.

proof_stages.label: Trapdoor matrix families and efficiency curves
proof_stages.summary: A trapdoor distribution over a matrix and a circuit is introduced: the matrix marginal is computationally indistinguishable from random, while the hidden circuit computes matrix-vector and related operations much faster than generic multiplication.

proof_stages.label: Generic substitution recipe for speedups
proof_stages.summary: Any polynomial-time algorithm that samples and later uses a random matrix can replace it by a trapdoor sample without changing observable behavior, and then replace matrix multiplications by the exact fast circuit to reduce running time.

proof_stages.label: Application template: random linear dimension reduction
proof_stages.summary: Random-projection algorithms, including Johnson-Lindenstrauss-type reductions and neural-network first layers, fit the template because their correctness proofs only require the projection to look random to the surrounding algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Application template: linear-algebra self-reductions
proof_stages.summary: Worst-case-to-average-case reductions for matrix multiplication and related linear-algebraic tasks are accelerated and strengthened by choosing the randomizing matrices from the trapdoor distribution, so that auxiliary products are computed cheaply while the oracle sees pseudorandom inputs.

proof_stages.label: LPN construction via hidden fast decompositions
proof_stages.summary: Trapdoor matrices are constructed by hiding decompositions into fast-multiplication pieces, with a simple LPN-based low-rank-plus-sparse construction giving an n^{1.5} matvec circuit and a slightly more elaborate product/sum decomposition giving nearly linear time.

proof_stages.label: General constructibility and pseudoaverage-case viewpoint
proof_stages.summary: The speaker argues that hiding small circuit size should be broadly possible under many hardness assumptions, and that reducing worst-case instances to computationally indistinguishable pseudoaverage distributions can evade known barriers to exact average-case reductions.

examples.name: Encryption/PRG secret-key intuition
examples.note: Encryption and pseudorandom generation illustrate that computational indistinguishability typically holds only without a secret key, motivating the new idea that a key could instead enable fast evaluation.

examples.name: Johnson-Lindenstrauss dimension reduction and variants
examples.note: JL projections, sparse JL transforms, and manifold/cluster/cut/MST-preserving variants show a familiar class of algorithms that sample a random linear map and can therefore use the trapdoor substitution template.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the trapdoor-matrix recipe to Johnson-Lindenstrauss-type random projections, discussing the target dimension, variants, failure of sparse JL in some settings, and how replacing the random matrix gives faster dimension reduction.

examples.name: BLR matrix-multiplication self-correction
examples.note: The classical four-query worst-case-to-average-case reduction exposes why trapdoor randomizers are useful: three dependent random multiplications become cheaply computable, leaving only one oracle call and allowing success probability epsilon.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through the BLR self-correction identity for matrix multiplication, expands the four terms, analyzes marginal randomness and failure probabilities, and then modifies the reduction using trapdoor matrices.

examples.name: Noisy matrix-multiplication error correction
examples.note: A matrix-multiplication algorithm whose output only slightly correlates with the truth motivates the symmetrization trick by random scalars and permutations, after which coordinatewise plurality removes the noise.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker develops the noisy matrix-multiplication correction in detail, defining the noise matrix, explaining repeated samples, enforcing nicer noise structure via random scalars and permutations, and recovering by coordinatewise plurality.

examples.name: Private inference compared with FHE
examples.note: Follow-up work on hiding matrices from a server during inference illustrates that the same trapdoor-matrix phenomenon can yield more practical cryptographic protocols than fully homomorphic encryption in this setting.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sketches how the trapdoor construction is used for private inference by masking a matrix so it looks random to the server and then peeling off the mask afterward, contrasting this mechanism with FHE.

passes.reason: private inference and FHE are used as a practical comparison rather than a refutation

Private inference is presented as a practical application and FHE as a baseline comparison, not as a mathematical counterexample.
Private inference with trapdoor matrices is contrasted with FHE as a practicality comparison rather than a mathematical refutation.
Private inference is presented as a practical application benchmarked against FHE rather than as a refuting example.
FHE is maximal in allowing arbitrary homomorphic computation, but here it is only a practical comparison point
